title: Dollhouse Bookcase
summary: A bookcase built as a peaked-roof house facade — mitered gable sides, working window and door trim, and shelves that double as floors.
description:
This bookcase gets its whole personality from two roof-peaked side panels: the tops of the two outer sides are cut at 30° to form a gable, and a matching center divider gets the same angle mitered down so the roofline reads correctly from the front. A miter saw makes these cuts exact — freehand, a 30° gable is the kind of angle that looks wrong the moment it's off by even a couple of degrees.

Windows and doors are cut into the side panels before assembly, while they're still flat and easy to clamp, and the trim frames around each opening are built as separate small units first, then centered and nailed into the cut opening afterward — much easier than trying to nail individual trim strips directly onto a vertical panel. If the trim is getting a different paint color than the case, finish the case and the trim frames separately before final assembly; painting a fitted trim frame in place, with a paintbrush working into inside corners, is a worse job every time.

Build the center section (the two vertical dividers and three shelves) as its own sub-assembly before attaching the outer gabled sides — get that center stack square and level first, since the outer sides register off it, not the other way around. The front footer trim and the mitered top pieces both rely on the case underneath already being true.

The scale here is genuinely a bookcase's — shelves that hold real books — dressed as a dollhouse facade rather than a literal dollhouse for play. It's a shelf-first piece of furniture with a house's face.

tools.note: Cuts the window and door openings in the side panels.

cutList.note: Top edge cut at a 30° angle to form the gable.
Top edge mitered down at 30° to follow the roofline.

steps.title: Cut the openings and roof angles
steps.body: Working from the diagram, cut the two side panels' top edges at 30° to form the gable roofline. From those same panels, cut out the window and door openings while the boards are still flat and easy to clamp — a miter saw will give you the exact roof angle, so the layout measurements only need to be close.

steps.title: Build the trim frames
steps.body:
Build the window and door trim as small standalone frames first, using 1-1/2" nails, rather than nailing individual strips directly onto the panel. Center each frame on its matching opening and nail into place.

If the trim is a different paint color than the case, finish the case and the trim frames separately now, before final assembly — it's much easier than painting inside corners once everything is nailed together.

steps.title: Build the center section
steps.body: Nail the two bottom vertical dividers to the three shelves first, then fasten the top vertical divider to the top shelf. Get this center stack square and level before moving on — the outer gabled sides register off this center section, not the other way around. Once the center is complete, nail the two gabled side panels on.

steps.title: Add the footer and top
steps.body:
Fasten the front footer trim to the front of the bookcase.

Then fasten the two top panels in place, nailing from each top piece into the opposite top piece at the peak of the gable end so the roofline joint is solid.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Fill nail holes with wood filler, sand the whole piece, and paint or finish as desired.

images.alt: Bookcase built to look like a peaked-roof dollhouse facade with windows and shelves
